title: Superconducting diode effect in magnetic superconductors realized by nonreciprocal domain-wall dynamics
authors: Dong Hui Han, Suk Bum Chung, Se Kwon Kim
abstract: A superconducting diode effect is shown to arise in ferromagnetic superconductors through the nonreciprocal dynamics of magnetic domain walls. Specifically, we show that current-driven dynamics of a magnetic domain wall under a certain external field can exhibit a nonreciprocal Walker breakdown, possessing two distinct direction-dependent critical currents beyond which the domain wall precesses continuously. In ferromagnetic superconductors, the constant rotation of a domain wall is shown to give rise to phase slips, opening up dissipation channels, whereby the nonreciprocal Walker breakdown is mapped to the superconducting diode effect. For the nonreciprocal Walker breakdown of a magnetic domain wall, we analytically examine its dependence on the magnetic field and the Gilbert damping and verify the theoretical results with micromagnetic simulations. We then extend the analysis to ferromagnetic superconductors by considering additional effects from the superconductivity and identify criteria for experimental conditions to realize the predicted superconducting diode effect. Our work demonstrates that topological defects, such as domain walls, in magnetic superconductors can serve as an intrinsic nanoscale platform for nonlinear nonreciprocal superconducting functionalities within a single homogeneous material, circumventing the need for complicated engineered heterostructures and thereby enabling the miniaturization of superconducting devices down to the nanometer scale that is challenging to achieve with conventional Josephson junctions.
summary: This paper theoretically establishes that the superconducting diode effect can arise intrinsically in ferromagnetic superconductors due to the nonreciprocal dynamics of magnetic domain walls. By analyzing the Walker breakdown mechanism, the authors derive criteria showing how this topological defect acts as a nanoscale platform for nonlinear superconducting functionalities without needing complex heterostructures.
main_problem: The study demonstrates how the superconducting diode effect (SDE) can emerge intrinsically from nonreciprocal dynamics of magnetic domain walls (DWs) within ferromagnetic superconductors (FMSCs). This mechanism circumvents the need for complex engineered heterostructures.
main_result: Nonreciprocal Walker breakdown in DW motion leads to asymmetric current-voltage characteristics, establishing a superconducting diode effect with a maximum efficiency ($\eta_{max}$) of 1/3. The analysis confirms that topological defects can serve as an intrinsic platform for nonlinear nonreciprocal functionalities.
method: The authors use analytical derivations based on the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equation and Ginzburg-Landau free energy functionals, extending models from simple ferromagnets to FMSCs via emergent gauge fields. These theoretical results are validated against micromagnetic simulations.
model_or_system: The system is a magnetic superconductor containing domain walls whose dynamics are governed by coupled equations derived from minimizing the superconducting and magnetic free energies. The model incorporates effects like pinning potentials, demagnetization, and spin-momentum coupling inherent to FMSCs.
key_observables: Superconducting Diode Effect (SDE) characterized by nonreciprocal critical supercurrent densities ($j_{\pm c}$) and diode efficiency ($\eta$).
important_parameters: External magnetic fields ($H_y, H_z$), Gilbert damping parameter ($\alpha$), pinning strength ($k_p$), and the condition for maximum efficiency ($H_{max} = \sqrt{2K_1}/\pi M$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on the adiabatic limit (neglecting nonadiabatic effects) and assumes specific field configurations ($H_y=H_z>0$) to maximize the effect.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematic representations of SDE via DW precession, I-V curves showing asymmetric behavior dependent on magnetic fields, and comparisons between theoretical predictions and micromagnetic simulations for $\eta$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the nonreciprocal Walker breakdown in simple ferromagnets, then extending this analysis to incorporate superconducting effects using a Ginzburg-Landau formalism, culminating in the derivation of criteria for realizing the SDE in FMSCs.
why_interesting: This work connects topological defects (DWs) and fundamental magnetic dynamics (LLG equation) directly to macroscopic quantum phenomena (superconductivity), providing a mechanism for spintronic functionality within a single material system, which is highly relevant for condensed matter theorists studying emergent gauge fields and non-equilibrium superconductivity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematics of the SDE induced by the DW precession ∆˙Φ in the nonreciprocal WB. In the thin film of width w and thickness d, the DW is pinned at a notch characterized by the geometric parameters nx and ny. The measured voltage V originates from the emergent electric fields [Eqs. (13) and (34)]. For the bias current of the equal magnitude, the volt- age V across the DW vanishes for one direction (right in the figure) and is finite for the other direction (left in the figure), thereby giving rise to the diode effect.

FIG. 2. I-V curves of a pinned DW in a notch obtained from simulations with Hy = Hz and β = 0. (a) shows the dependence on the magnetic field intensity where α = 0.2. The solid lines represent the numerical solutions from the EOMs [Eqs. (17) and (18)], which are used to determine kp = 0.45 × 10−3 N/m. (b) shows the dependence on α where Hy = Hz = 30 Oe. The solid lines represent ⟨V ⟩= −2P⟨˙Φ⟩ [Eqs. (13) and (19)]. For α = 0.15 and α = 0.2, the DW is depinned from the notch at jdep = 1.74 × 1011A/m2 and jdep = 2.35 × 1011A/m2, respectively.

FIG. 3. Plots of the diode efficiency η [Eq. (7)] for various magnetic field conditions. (a) Numerical results obtained from the EOMs [Eqs. (5) and (6)] for |Hy|, |Hz| ≤100 Oe, where α = 0.2 and β = 0. The black dashed lines indicate contours of η = 0, 0.1, 0.2. (b) and (c) present comparisons with micromagnetic simulations for the black dash-dotted line Hy = Hz > 0 and the red dash-dotted line (Hy, Hz) = √
FIG. 4. I-V curves of a pinned DW for various β obtained from simulations with Hy = Hz = 30 Oe and α = 0.2. The black solid line represents ⟨V ⟩= −2P⟨˙Φ⟩[Eqs. (13) and (19)].

FIG. 5. I-V curves showing the effect of demagnetization on the precessional dynamics of a DW pinned at a notch. Simulations are performed with Hy = Hz = 226 Oe and M = 40 kA/m, while all other material parameters are the same as those used in the main text. The square symbols rep- resent the case including the demagnetization effect, whereas the circular symbols correspond to the case neglecting demag- netization but including local hard-axis anisotropy. The black solid line represents ⟨V ⟩= −2P⟨˙Φ⟩[Eqs. (13) and (19)].
